--- render_with_liquid: false --- Collaborative Mortgage Scoring

The Federated Data Science Lifecycle

This notebook explores the four phases of the federated data science lifecycle. We start with collaboration setup — creating a shared project and registering each lender's dataset under governance controls. From there, we move into federated analytics, profiling both portfolios through counts, means, distributions, and correlations without any raw data movement. With a clear picture of the data, we enter federated learning: preparing each site's data in-situ, training XGBoost models via NVIDIA FLARE, and evaluating performance using ROC, F1, F2, balanced accuracy, and confusion matrices. Finally, we close the loop with federated inference — packaging the trained global model into a portable container and deploying it to each lender's environment, where predictions run locally and no applicant record ever leaves the institution that owns it.

Phase 1: Collaboration Setup

Authenticate to the Rhino API

Enter your credentials below. Update the constants to match your project and dataset names. In Rhino, a project is the collaboration container — it defines which institutions participate, what governance rules apply, and which datasets and code objects can be shared within the collaboration.

import rhino_health as rh
from getpass import getpass
from rhino_sdk_utils import *

PROJECT_NAME = "Collaborative Mortgage Scoring"  # ← update
LENDER_A_DATASET = "Dataset A"  # ← update
LENDER_B_DATASET = "Dataset B"  # ← update
TEST_DATASET = "Dataset B"  # ← update
CODE_OBJECT_NAME = "XGBoost 2.7"  # ← update

USERNAME = "sam.smith@projectlead.org"
PASSWORD = getpass()  # Replace with your Rhino Health username
session = rh.login(username=USERNAME, rhino_api_url=rh.ApiEnvironment.SOLUTIONS_GCP_URL, password=PASSWORD)

# Correct way to find a project by name
projects = session.project.get_projects()
project = next((p for p in projects if p.name == PROJECT_NAME), None)
print(f"Project: {project.name}  ({str(project.uid)})")

You are not using the latest version of the Rhino SDK.
Latest version: 2.1.23
Current version: 2.1.22
To upgrade, run: pip install --upgrade rhino_health
Project: Collaborative Mortgage Scoring  (e41363b4-8efb-4df4-8bd3-a27cf6506b18)

lender_a = session.dataset.get_dataset_by_name(LENDER_A_DATASET, project_uid=str(project.uid))
lender_b = session.dataset.get_dataset_by_name(LENDER_B_DATASET, project_uid=str(project.uid))

all_uids = [str(lender_a.uid), str(lender_b.uid)]
print(f"Lender 1: {lender_a.name}  ({str(lender_a.uid)})")
print(f"Lender 2: {lender_b.name}  ({str(lender_b.uid)})")
Lender 1: Dataset A  (f93c1d4a-dffb-4509-b428-3b78526f2d73)
Lender 2: Dataset B  (dcda22de-7377-47e6-93f8-03893f9bf94d)

Phase 2: Federated Analytics

Exploratory Data Analysis — Count Rows & Examine Column Means

Before any modeling, we profile each lender's portfolio using federated analytics. Rhino sends a statistical query to each institution; aggregate counts and means are computed locally on the raw applicant records and only the summary values return to the orchestrator. No individual loan record, income figure, or default outcome ever leaves the institution that holds it.

This is the core value of federated analytics: visualize your collaborator's data without data transfer.

summary = fetch_portfolio_summary(session, lender_a, lender_b, all_uids)
print(summary)

⟳  Communicating with federated clients...
✓  Portfolio summary retrieved.
{'Lender A': {'Applicants': 140391, 'Mean Income ($)': 50959.13413252986, 'Mean Loan Amount ($)': 9352.489547050736, 'Default Rate': 0.33096478009149427}, 'Lender B': {'Applicants': 218492, 'Mean Income ($)': 84350.88698899731, 'Mean Loan Amount ($)': 12160.657232301412, 'Default Rate': 0.12292440913168445}, 'Federated': {'Applicants': 358883, 'Mean Income ($)': 71288.40819988686, 'Mean Loan Amount ($)': 11062.133564420716, 'Default Rate': 0.20430746633812405}}

Examine Distributions

Do the two lenders serve the same type of borrower? We look at age distribution, employment tenure, and income bracket to understand whether these are two slices of the same population — or two genuinely different risk pools.

age_a, age_b = fetch_age_bands(session, lender_a, lender_b)
income_a, income_b = fetch_income_brackets(session, lender_a, lender_b)
plot_demographics(age_a, age_b, income_a, income_b)
✓  Age distribution retrieved (7 bands)                              
✓  Income distribution retrieved (8 brackets)

Multivariate Correlation Analysis

We compute pairwise Pearson correlations for 8 key variables — once per lender and once federated. The federation heatmap may reveal correlations that are too weak to detect in either portfolio alone, or demonstrate that characteristics associated with loan default may be different within each population.

CORR_VARS = [
    "person_age",
    "person_income",
    "person_emp_length",
    "loan_amnt",
    "loan_int_rate",
    "loan_percent_income",
    "cb_person_cred_hist_length",
    "loan_status",
]
CORR_LABELS = [
    "Age",
    "Income",
    "Emp. Length",
    "Loan Amount",
    "Int. Rate",
    "Loan/Income %",
    "Credit History",
    "Default",
]

corr_a = build_corr_matrix(session, str(lender_a.uid), CORR_VARS, "Lender A")
corr_b = build_corr_matrix(session, str(lender_b.uid), CORR_VARS, "Lender B")
plot_correlation_heatmaps(corr_a, corr_b, CORR_LABELS)
✓  Lender A correlation matrix complete (28 pairs)                                    █████
✓  Lender B correlation matrix complete (28 pairs)                                    █████

Phase 3: Federated Learning

Data Preparation — Outlier Identification Using Standard Deviation

Before training, we prepare each lender's data in-situ. Using federated statistics, we compute per-feature mean and standard deviation across both lenders without exchanging any raw records. These bounds are then used to remove outlier applicant rows at each site independently.

NUMERIC_FEATURES = [
    "person_age",
    "person_income",
    "person_emp_length",
    "loan_amnt",
    "loan_int_rate",
    "loan_percent_income",
    "cb_person_cred_hist_length",
]
TEST_FRACTION = 0.2

bounds = compute_outlier_bounds(session, all_uids, NUMERIC_FEATURES, zscore_threshold=3.0)
display_outlier_bounds(bounds)
✓  Federated outlier bounds computed (7 features)                                   ength
Federated 3σ Outlier Bounds — aggregated across both lender sites
  Lower Bound (μ − 3σ) Upper Bound (μ + 3σ)
Feature    
person_age 10.95 45.67
person_income -73,869.83 216,446.65
person_emp_length -11.78 21.81
loan_amnt -10,555.88 32,680.15
loan_int_rate 0.90 21.49
loan_percent_income -0.34 0.78
cb_person_cred_hist_length -16.65 39.31

Outlier Removal & Stratified Train/Test Split

Any applicant row that falls outside the 3σ bounds for one or more numeric features is excluded. We then split the cleaned data into stratified train and test sets, preserving the default-rate distribution in each split. Both steps run in a single Rhino code object so only one container execution is needed per lender.

data_prep_code = build_data_prep_snippet(bounds, TEST_FRACTION)
deploy_and_run_snippet(
    session,
    project,
    "Outlier Removal & Test/Train Split",
    data_prep_code,
    datasets=[lender_a, lender_b],
    output_templates=[
        [f"{LENDER_A_DATASET} - Train", f"{LENDER_A_DATASET} - Test"],
        [f"{LENDER_B_DATASET} - Train", f"{LENDER_B_DATASET} - Test"],
    ],
    requirements=["pandas", "scikit-learn"],
)
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — deploying 'Outlier Removal & Test/Train Split'...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — running on site 1/2...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — running on site 2/2...
✓  New datasets successfully prepared; ready for processing.

Index Newly-Created Train & Test Sets

Each lender's dataset has been cleaned and partitioned entirely within its own environment. The resulting train and test sets are registered as derivative datasets in the Rhino project and can now be referenced by downstream workflows. No data has moved between the two lenders at any point in the preparation pipeline.

lender_a_train, lender_b_train, lender_a_test, lender_b_test = fetch_datasets(
    session,
    project,
    [
        f"{LENDER_A_DATASET} - Train",
        f"{LENDER_B_DATASET} - Train",
        f"{LENDER_A_DATASET} - Test",
        f"{LENDER_B_DATASET} - Test",
    ],
)
train_uids = [str(lender_a_train.uid), str(lender_b_train.uid)]
test_uids = [str(lender_a_test.uid), str(lender_b_test.uid)]
print("Train/test split complete ✓")

Train/test split complete ✓

Model Training — NVIDIA FLARE Federated Learning

We now train a shared XGBoost gradient-boosted tree model using NVIDIA FLARE federated learning. Each lender trains locally on their own data and contributes only model updates — never raw records. After 10 federation rounds, the global model has seen patterns from both portfolios without either institution sharing a single applicant record.

import json

train_matrix = {
    "Site A Model": [lender_a_train.uid],
    "Site B Model": [lender_b_train.uid],
    "Global Model": [lender_a_train.uid, lender_b_train.uid],
}

with open("xgboost_flare/app/config/config_fed_client.json") as f:
    client_config = json.load(f)
with open("xgboost_flare/app/config/config_fed_server.json") as f:
    server_config = json.load(f)

code_runs = run_federated_training(
    session, project, CODE_OBJECT_NAME, train_matrix, client_config, server_config
)
print("All training runs complete ✓")
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — dispatching 3 training run(s)...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — waiting on 'Site A Model' [1/3]...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — waiting on 'Site B Model' [2/3]...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — waiting on 'Global Model' [3/3]...
✓  All 3 training run(s) complete.
All training runs complete ✓

Model Evaluation — ROC Curve

With training complete, we evaluate each model using the Rhino SDK's federated evaluation tools. Inference runs locally at each site — the trained model travels to the data, not the other way around. We compare three variants: a model trained on Lender A's data alone, one trained on Lender B's data alone, and the federated global model that learned from both portfolios without either lender sharing raw records.

test_matrix = {
    "Site A Test Set": [lender_a_test.uid],
    "Site B Test Set": [lender_b_test.uid],
}

inference_results = run_inference_matrix(session, code_runs, test_matrix)
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — inference [1/4] Site A Model → Site A Test Set...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — inference [2/4] Site B Model → Site B Test Set...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — inference [3/4] Global Model → Site A Test Set...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — inference [4/4] Global Model → Site B Test Set...
✓  All 4 inference run(s) complete.

metrics = compute_evaluation_metrics(session, inference_results)

plot_roc_curves(metrics)
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — computing evaluation metrics...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — metrics for 'Site A Model' [1/3]...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — metrics for 'Site B Model' [2/3]...
⟳  Communicating with federated clients — metrics for 'Global Model' [3/3]...
✓  Evaluation metrics computed for 3 model(s).

Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and Confusion Matrix

The ROC curve summarizes discrimination ability across all thresholds, but lenders set a single operating threshold for underwriting decisions. Below, we evaluate each model at that threshold using F1, F2 (which weights recall more heavily to penalize missed defaults), balanced accuracy, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient. The confusion matrices show where each model tends to approve risky applicants versus reject creditworthy ones — a tradeoff each institution weighs according to its own risk appetite.

plot_scalar_metrics_and_confusion(metrics)

Phase 4: Federated Inference

Packaging the Global Model as an Edge Inference Container

With training complete and the global model validated, the final step is packaging it for deployment to each lender's edge environment. This preserves the same privacy guarantee as training: the model travels to the data, not the other way around.

The inference container is a self-contained Docker image that:

  1. Accepts a dataset of applicant records (identical schema, without the loan_status label column)
  2. Runs the trained global XGBoost model locally at each lender's site — no data leaves the institution
  3. Returns the original dataset augmented with two new columns: scores (model probability, 0–1) and predicted_class (binary prediction at a 0.5 threshold)

The container is built once and deployed across both lenders via Rhino FCP's container distribution layer. Each site runs inference entirely within its own environment. Rhino's utilities handle containerization automatically — teams can also configure CI/CD pipelines for model versioning using GitHub Actions.

For teams deploying custom images: Rhino provides a docker_push.sh helper script (available in /user-resources on the platform) that builds and pushes Docker images from a local machine to the Rhino-managed AWS ECR registry. From there, images can be referenced in code objects just like any auto-built container.

inference_container = create_inference_container(
    session, project, code_runs["Global Model"], container_path="inference_container/"
)
⟳  Extracting trained model parameters...

inference_result = run_production_inference(
    session, inference_container, test_datasets=[lender_a_test, lender_b_test]
)

Summary

This demo walked end-to-end through a federated machine learning workflow for credit risk — from raw applicant data to a production-ready inference container — without a single raw record leaving either institution.

Phase What Rhino Did Privacy Guarantee
EDA Computed portfolio statistics, distributions, and correlations across both lenders Only aggregate summaries returned — no individual records
Data Prep Derived outlier bounds federally; ran cleaning and train/test splits in-situ Bounds computed without pooling data; splits executed locally
Training Ran 10 rounds of NVIDIA FLARE federated XGBoost across both lenders Only model weight updates exchanged — never raw features or labels
Evaluation Scored all three model variants against both test sets; computed ROC, F1, MCC Inference ran at each site; only metric summaries aggregated
Deployment Packaged the global model into a portable container; distributed to each lender's edge Container runs locally — predictions stay within each institution

The federated global model outperforms both site-specific models — demonstrating that institutions can collaborate to improve predictive accuracy while fully preserving data sovereignty.
